speaker: interviewer
text: --before in the interview with Edward Polidi. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we arrive to this moment, in which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was born my new sister. She was born in 1945, the 10th of December. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the time, I already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was very intensively playing my violin. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And what I remember was that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] time to time, when I was playing the violin, I was seeing [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] crying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And during this time-- how I mentioned my father was mostly outside of the city, traveling to provide the money for survival. And she was working at home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the climate, the, the atmosphere was very-- basically, very depressed, depressed situation. I started to go in regular school, started my studies. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] immediately, something-- what happened kind of, for a little while, refreshed the climate inside of my house. This was that I was accepted as a youngest member-- or from that time-- for children orchestra in the capital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Not only that, but I very soon become not only the concertmaster there, but also the soloist of this orchestra. This was one side of my life. The same time were happening the other thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Soon as my new sister was born, my--

speaker: interviewer
text: What name was she given?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- her name was-- she was given the name [NON-ENGLISH] Margareta. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. She was-- my mother became very badly depressed, very, very sick. She was-- I was seeing her all the time in one very badly crying mood. Until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just the time of my birthday, which was 23rd of December, they came-- some of the relative, in which I had to perform a concert my little violin. And the same night, my mother suicides, the night after my birthday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many things started to change, of course, in this situation. The first things what-- concern was-- basically was with the baby. And this baby was taken by the-- my relatives, the sisters of my mother, and given to family, the-- just at that time was preparing their emigration to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [SIGHS] I continued my studies. And this was kind of two lines of my life that are going simultaneously. One line was the tragedy of my family. My father become even more depressed and secluded. And it's not very far after that, he, he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other side, my successes as a young musician. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] become [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stipendium bearer. I mean, I start to receive stipendium from the, the government for my studies. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I finish the lyceum, the-- with top grades there at the time. And little before that I finished the lyceum, there was one competition-- national competition inside of the country in which I took the golden medal and first prize.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 years old. [SIGHS] This is what eventually after that prepare my first big trip outside of the country. Of course, immediately after that, I, I had to finish lyceum and to enter in the state conservatory as part of the university. I, I, I started there, started my studies in the Bulgarian Conservatory. Who Who were you-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --living with? Who had raised you to this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living with very close cousins of mine. But once I entered in university, I took my own small apartment because I was receiving scholarship. I received a scholarship that was the highest scholarship. In the entrance of my examination, I received this very special-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here would say A with two pluses in my entrance examination, which allowed me immediately to receive after that very high special scholarship. This was one of the good thing that the government of the socialist country used to provide-- conditions for the education of the young people that are very favorable for their development. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was able, in this time, already, to start build my own life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Very close to the end, I took the last few years in one. I took all the examination in order that I have my, my first baccalaureate bachelor-- finished there. And I win [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one internal audition for going to study in Moscow.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I studied there as a [NON-ENGLISH], meaning external student, something like post-graduate studies, with one of the greatest teachers that they had in the time, David Oistrakh. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had possibility to take some lessons in composition with Dmitri Shostakovich. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I stay in Russia approximately three years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Upon my return, I-- in the beginning, I become assistant professor in the conservatory of Sofia. And later on, parallel with that, I become one of the concert artist of the concert direction of Bulgaria. They had this kind of direction that this-- only one, and controlled by the state. We used to give concerts in a number of cities inside with other not only instrumentalists, but also vocalists. They came from the-- soloists from the opera. And like that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] organizing or arranging a number of multimedia concerts in different portion of Bulgaria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, when came, however, after a certain period of time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what happened-- happened something very strange. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since I had some cousins that were living outside of the country-- I had one, my uncle, living in Paris. He was brother of my father, younger brother of my father. His name was Marcel [NON-ENGLISH] Polidi. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When he understood that he have one very talented nephew, he organized one concert of mine in Paris in Salle Gaveau. Even I received in my home copy of the arrangements, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they never allow me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is 'they'?

speaker: interviewee
text: The government, meaning concert direction. That one half of the concert direction was the police part. They were under the control of the police, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which country are we speaking of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Bulgaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I already returned back. The second event that happened in that time was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Bulgaria, when the time came one of the greatest conductors of our time, Herman Abendroth. He was the conductor of the Berlin Radio, Radio Symphony Orchestra. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He heard me playing when he was visiting in Bulgaria. And I received invitation to become second concertmaster in his orchestra in Berlin. Again, by unknown reasons, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they sold this contract that he sended from there. Everything disappear. Was sended a number of letters there. And all the time, the reply was that I was deadly sick, very badly sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who replied--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know that-- the concert direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the auspices of the police. I mean, it's-- after many, many years, I understood actually that this happened. Because to me, they used to say, no, you never received anything. Or that this agreement is invalid, and they are not able to allow me to travel to France where my uncle arranged a concerts. Because it's very complicated. And I have what to do in Bulgaria, that I am not allowed because the conservatory is-- supposedly, is not allowing-- grant me a, a time to go to take time off from my teachings. With one or other words, very soon become clear to me that I am becoming [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very black stain. My dossier there, in the police, is receiving this kind of status in which the country become one prison for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is when I decided to, to leave the country there. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the attitude toward all musicians in Bulgaria at the time? Or do you think there--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was a reason it was happening to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I was of Jewish descendance. Secondly, I-- my descendance was from bourgeois descendance, not from proletariat descendance. Thirdly, there was somebody in political circle that wanted to promote somebody else because they sended someone else in my place when I was invited.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this person, after three months, was sent back. We don't want this. Where is Edward Polidi? This is a person that we contracted. So obviously, there was this tremendous element of political maneuvering and arrangements.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, when one person there is gifted, and it's not their-- of their own-- always produced some kind of jealousy and some kind of feelings, negative feelings that, if they have the opportunity, they will eventually show that and will give open green light of one so-called their persons. Not one of-- I'm-- now, when I go out, I'm not representing their country because I don't have the Bulgarian name, which is [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Edward Polidi, a Jewish person of our country. So this is politically not correct. It's politically wrong. And this, in the socialist Bulgaria, obviously at the time was one-- some factor, bigger or smaller factor. But nevertheless, when I applied to leave, I was thrown out of the university. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was called from the police quarters there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there-- big scandal. How is possible that I want to leave the country? And that they all the time knew that, which was absolutely false, was not true. I didn't have this idea before. They put it in my mind-- that I am with tied hands and arrested there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So then I confirm these things that I want to leave. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put me back in the army. I should say that when I was studying there, I was obliged to go also-- parallel with my musical study, also to military study. I went to see so-called this [NON-ENGLISH] 22. That was military [NON-ENGLISH]. And I ended with the position of lieutenant of the army, Bulgarian Army. I, I was excelling in very precise shooting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they took my-- away my-- they put me in the regular army, almost like in prison. And I was denied the-- leaving the country. And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the year of '57, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '58. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, no, it's when I left-- I'm making mistake-- '62, '62, '63. So it's when I-- first time left, yes. It's after that I returned back. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the end, I was granted. And this is when I left to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Open new page, totally new page. I-- first of all, by when I came there, I kissed the earth. It was pretty-- a rainy day, pretty much mud on the earth. I went there with one very special feeling that I am arriving to the earth of my ancestors. And in despite very difficult conditions that I-- first of all, I went there to-- on kibbutz, working the earth for a number of months and learning the language that I find out that I don't know absolutely at all-- not very easy language. And I succeed to learn to write and read pretty fluently. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until the moment in which I had the possibility to get connected again with the big cities, with Tel Aviv, and to make some connections that permitted me later on to make my auditions in which I was accepted as a member of the very famous-- worldly famous Israel Philharmonic Orchestra. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- this was my title. I was starting from the very back of this section of the second violin section. I arrived very soon up to the first stand. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I remained in Israel for a few years [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I find out that I'm able to participate in other audition, this allowing me not only to, to learn of some other very great masters, but would permit me to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do something that I was, all my life, dreaming to be able to concertize. This, in Israel, was very difficult, especially if you would like to live as a musician, do this concertize-- because the country is very small. This is the reason why you, you see all the time the greatest talent of Israel leaving Israel and arranging their life here, like Perlman-- Itzhak Perlman, Zukerman, and many others-- many others, more or less known people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I succeed to win a scholarship-- full scholarship for the Accademia di Santa Cecilia in Italy, in Rome. And this is where I went from Israel-- there. And then I become, in Rome, concertmaster of Rome Opera. So I stay there also for a number of years, until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that it happened that they came to the United States for performances. The Rome Opera came here. And I received here offer from Metropolitan Opera in New York. What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time-- this was the year of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '68, '68, '69. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From the general manager at the time, Rudolf Bing, to join Metropolitan Opera-- it's much better conditions, in every respect, in every respect. First of all, there, I was not able to-- never to really receive the citizen-- Italian citizenship in one normal way. Here, was supposed-- a different situation altogether. Besides that, the conditions-- economic conditions-- totally different. And one other thing that I find out that I-- was fascinating at the time was that-- I find out that in New York, which is a much more international city, I felt much more at home than in once-- Italian city, in which the nationalistic element-- the national element is much more powerful, much stronger. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here, I agreed again to move my life. I came to United States. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This new page open now with opening new possibilities my life. I still never lost my desire to concertize. One thing I should mention that I didn't mention before was that meanwhile-- during this period happened one very important event in my life, very, very important event of one-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] major importance. During my studies and, and stay and work in Italy, I made it at the competitions. I was able to make other competitions, which I win again prizes, high prizes. One was one competition in Italy. I win there the first price of the Accademia Chigiana in Italy. And then, very soon after that, I win the special prize of George Enescu International Competition in Romania. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At the time, I was still citizen of Israel-- Israeli citizen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What happened was that I was accepted because they make a selection of whom they are able to accept for these auditions. I was accepted as only one representative of, meanwhile, the new state of Israel. I was the only one violinist competing of Israeli-- the new Israeli State. Of course, in the beginning, nobody didn't know that. But soon as-- after that first round was over-- because they two rounds-- selective rounds, in which they, after that, select the first prize, second, third, fourth, and-- et cetera. My name started to appear in the newspapers there. And here immediately come in clear lights that there is one Israeli musician competitor in this. And especially after the second circle-- second tour, when I was selected for the finals, so the semi-finals, again the name of Israel was there, showing up. I start to receive tremendous amount of phones in my hotels. I should say before that that my hotel was selected in a different location than the hotel of the contestants that were coming from socialist country, including from my own native country. There were so-called socialistic hotels-- all the Russians, Czechoslovakian, Polish. And I was in the capitalistic world--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were hotels different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel-- which-- there were some Americans there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the way, the Americans, a number of them were of Israeli descendents-- France, England, Germany, and some other countries. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I start to receive these calls during the night. They didn't stop all the night. But this-- not, not only the night, but also the following. So this obviously attracted attention of the KGB in Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, these calls stopped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very glad, between us, that they stopped. Because the last night, I really needed one good night's sleep. [LAUGHS] Because the competition is almost like the Olympics. You need to be in, in maximum shape-- biological, physical, and mental shape when you are playing-- performing there are on the highest world level, in front of the top authorities of the world. This was-- jury very similar to the jury that was-- given to the American very famous now pianist, Van Cliburn, the first prize on the piano competition, with the greatest composers, greatest artists, performers, all over the world.
